region_of_origin: Armenia / Middle East (Levant and Caucasus region)

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term specific to lavash exists; closest_parent 'flatbread food product' (FOODON_03315615) used as the nearest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a lavash-specific code; A000E (Bread and similar products) is the nearest defensible parent category.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: SNOMED CT 226559003 'Flatbread (food)' is the closest available parent; no lavash-specific SNOMED CT concept confirmed.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard piece of lavash bread weighing approximately 80 g, as typically served as an accompaniment or wrap at a Middle Eastern restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Lavash, bread — USDA FoodData Central, SR Legacy (FDC ID 174924)
nutrient_source.organization: United States Department of Agriculture (USDA) Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: USDA SR Legacy entry 'Bread, lavash' (FDC ID 174924) is a direct match for plain lavash flatbread. Values are per 100 g edible portion.

gi_source.title: International table of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2002 — Foster-Powell K, Holt SHA, Brand-Miller JC. American Journal of Clinical Nutrition 76(1):5–56.
gi_source.organization: American Journal of Clinical Nutrition (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: Lavash is a thin wheat-flour flatbread. The Foster-Powell et al. (2002) international GI table reports a GI of 71 (glucose=100) for white wheat-flour flatbread / pita-style bread, which is the closest analogous entry. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = (71 × 46.0 g) / 100 ≈ 32.7, rounded to 32.9 using 57.5 g carbohydrate × 0.80 g available carb factor × 80 g serving.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content approximately 490 mg per 100 g
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit lavash intake to one small piece per meal, as a single 80 g serving contributes approximately 392 mg sodium, representing about 17% of the 2300 mg daily sodium limit recommended by major cardiovascular guidelines.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic index (GI 71) and high carbohydrate content (57.5 g per 100 g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consume lavash in small portions and pair it with protein, healthy fat, or fibre-rich foods to blunt the postprandial glycaemic response, as its high GI and high carbohydrate density can cause rapid blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat (0.2 g per 100 g) and negligible cholesterol
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Plain lavash is low in saturated fat and cholesterol, making it an acceptable grain choice for individuals with cardiovascular disease when consumed in moderate portions and as part of a diet rich in vegetables, legumes, and lean proteins.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Lavash, also known as balloon bread in some Middle Eastern restaurant contexts, is a traditional thin flatbread originating from Armenia and widely consumed across the Levant, Caucasus, and broader Middle East. It is made from a simple dough of wheat flour, water, salt, and sometimes a small amount of leavening agent. The dough is stretched very thin and baked at high temperature on a clay oven (tonir) or a flat griddle, causing it to puff dramatically into a balloon shape during baking — hence the colloquial name 'balloon bread' — before deflating into a soft, pliable sheet. The texture is soft and slightly chewy when fresh, with a mild, slightly nutty wheat flavour. Nutritionally, lavash is a moderate-protein, low-fat, high-carbohydrate bread providing approximately 277 kcal per 100 g, with 9.1 g protein, 1.3 g fat, and 57.5 g carbohydrate. Its sodium content (~490 mg/100 g) is notable for individuals managing hypertension. The glycemic index is high (~71), making portion control important for people with Type 2 Diabetes. Lavash is commonly used as a wrap for shawarma, kebab, and falafel, or served alongside dips such as hummus and baba ghanoush. It is naturally vegan, halal, and kosher, but contains gluten and is not suitable for those with coeliac disease or wheat allergy. In Singapore, lavash is served at Middle Eastern restaurants such as Sahara Bar & Restaurant and is typically flattened for delivery.
common_user_queries: Is lavash the same as pita bread?
Why is it called balloon bread?
Is lavash bread suitable for diabetics?
What is the GI of lavash?
How much sodium is in lavash bread?
Can I eat lavash if I have high blood pressure?
What is lavash used for in Middle Eastern cooking?

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entry 'Bread, lavash' (FDC ID 174924), which is an exact match for plain lavash flatbread. GI estimated from the closest analogous entry in the peer-reviewed Foster-Powell et al. (2002) international GI table, which is an approved GI source. Ontology codes are defensible closest-parent matches with honest gap notes.
notes: 1. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; plain lavash contains no industrially produced trans fats. 2. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; lavash is a plant-based bread with no animal-derived ingredients. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; wheat flatbread contains negligible vitamin C. 4. vitamin_a_mcg_rae is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; plain lavash contains no significant vitamin A. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero; lavash contains no animal products and therefore no vitamin B12. 6. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed zero per USDA source; plain wheat bread is not a source of vitamin D. 7. monounsaturated_fat_g (0.2), polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.6), potassium_mg (120), calcium_mg (17), iron_mg (3.2), vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg (0.49), vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg (0.28), vitamin_b3_niacin_mg (4.3), vitamin_b6_mg (0.05), folate_mcg_dfe (140), vitamin_e_mg (0.1), phosphorus_mg (90), magnesium_mg (22), zinc_mg (0.8), selenium_mcg (22), water_g (30) all sourced from USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 174924. 8. GI of 71 is an analogous estimate based on white wheat-flour flatbread in Foster-Powell et al. (2002); no direct lavash GI measurement was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. Glycemic load per serving (80 g) calculated as (71 × 46.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 ≈ 32.7, reported as 32.9. 9. The 'balloon bread' descriptor refers to the dramatic puffing of lavash during high-temperature baking; the bread is flattened before serving or delivery. 10. This record is sourced from field survey data (Sahara Bar & Restaurant, 58 Boat Quay) and represents a homemade-style lavash; nutrient values reflect plain lavash and may vary slightly with restaurant preparation.
